in_text.heading: Understanding Recurrent Ascites
in_text.statement: Recurrent ascites is characterized by repeated accumulation of fluid in the abdominal cavity. Symptoms may include abdominal distension, discomfort, and shortness of breath.

in_text.heading: What Are Your Treatment Options?
in_text.statement: Compare your options for managing recurrent ascites.

in_text.heading: Large Volume Paracentesis
in_text.statement: Large volume paracentesis (LVP) involves insertion of a needle into the peritoneal cavity to drain excess fluid and provide temporary relief from recurrent ascites.

in_text.heading: Large Volume Paracentesis
in_text.statement: Outpatient procedure performed in a hospital

in_text.heading: Large Volume Paracentesis
in_text.statement: Repetitive procedure required whenever fluid builds up impairing QoL

in_text.heading: Large Volume Paracentesis
in_text.statement: Up to 97 mins required per drainage

in_text.heading: Diuretics
in_text.statement: Diuretics are medications that increase excretion of sodium and water, aiming to achieve a negative sodium balance and reduce fluid accumulation in the abdominal cavity.

in_text.heading: Diuretics
in_text.statement: Oral administration, especially for ascites from liver cirrhosis (requires careful assessment of kidney function, risk of hypovolemia, and ototoxicity)

in_text.heading: Diuretics
in_text.statement: Administered once or twice daily

in_text.heading: Diuretics
in_text.statement: Regular monitoring required for electrolyte imbalance or kidney damage

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: An indwelling peritoneal catheter is a long-term option for managing recurrent ascites, allowing patients to drain excess fluid at home. This supports greater independence and improved quality of life.

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: One-time outpatient procedure to place catheter

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: Fewer hospitalization days improving QoL

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: Drainage performed at home

in_text.heading: Large Volume Paracentesis
in_text.statement: Procedure Setting: Outpatient or hospital-based procedure

in_text.heading: Diuretics
in_text.statement: Procedure Setting: Prescribed after assessment of body weight, fluid status, renal function and electrolytes

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: Procedure Setting: One-time outpatient catheter insertion

in_text.heading: Large Volume Paracentesis
in_text.statement: Frequency of Use: Requires repeated procedures each time fluid reaccumulates

in_text.heading: Diuretics
in_text.statement: Frequency of Use: Daily, long-term therapy

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: Frequency of Use: Repeated drainage at home as needed

in_text.heading: Large Volume Paracentesis
in_text.statement: Hospital Visits: Hospital/clinic visits each time fluid builds up

in_text.heading: Diuretics
in_text.statement: Hospital Visits: Regular monitoring of electrolytes & kidney function

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: Hospital Visits: Hospital visits not required once catheter in place

in_text.heading: Large Volume Paracentesis
in_text.statement: Cost: - Procedure cost - Cost of multiple hospital admissions

in_text.heading: Diuretics
in_text.statement: Cost: Medication cost Medical supervision cost

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: Cost: - Procedure cost for catheter insertion Drainage bottle cost

in_text.heading: Large Volume Paracentesis
in_text.statement: Invasiveness: Minimally invasive bedside procedure

in_text.heading: Diuretics
in_text.statement: Invasiveness: Non-invasive (oral medication)

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: Invasiveness: Minimally invasive insertion

in_text.heading: Large Volume Paracentesis
in_text.statement: Pain or Side Effects: Hypotension, wound infection, leakage of fluid at the needle insertion site

in_text.heading: Diuretics
in_text.statement: Pain or Side Effects: Electrolyte imbalances, dehydration, hypotension, ototoxicity, renal dysfunction

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: Pain or Side Effects: Infection and catheter blockage

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: How does it work?: Minimally invasive placement: A small tube is inserted through a tiny abdomen incision during a simple outpatient procedure.

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: How does it work?: Simple home drainage: When fluid builds up, connect the tube to a vacuum bottle. Each session takes 5-15 minutes.

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: How does it work?: Continuous symptom control: Drain fluid at home, reducing hospital visits and improving comfort.

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: 100% catheter placement success

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: 76% had no hospital visits due to catheter-related complications.

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: 83% to 100% of patients enjoyed relief from ascites symptoms.

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: Infection rate as low as 6%.

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: The catheter is inserted in the abdomen for draining ascites.

in_text.heading: Indwelling Peritoneal Catheter
in_text.statement: The protective dressing is virtually undetectable under clothing.
